introduction: In this final Capstone Project, you will step into the shoes of a DevOps Engineer tasked with deploying and securing a production environment. You will pull together all the individual concepts from the previous modules: navigating the directory tree, configuring restrictive permissions, writing automated bash utility scripts, managing system service accounts, and auditing active network sockets.

This is not a theoretical exercise. You will build a simulated deployment workflow: creating the workspace structure under a dedicated path, writing server configurations, automating log rotations, running script services in the background, redirecting outputs, and verifying port bounds.

This module serves as the final integration benchmark. Success in this module demonstrates that you are ready to transition to container orchestrations with Docker, cloud infrastructure with Terraform, and Kubernetes cluster deployments.
what_you_will_learn: Configuring secure permission profiles on server settings.
Deploying application scripts in background daemon mode.
Auditing TCP sockets and active system jobs listings.
overview.prerequisites: Completion of Linux Networking & Processes course.
learning_outcomes: Deploy a complete, secured application framework in Linux.
Enforce least-privilege permissions boundaries across files and processes.
Create automated scripts that rotate and clear log files.
Audit active ports and verify background daemon execution status.

theory.title: Production Deployment Workspace Architectures
theory.definition: Structuring folders and service accounts according to the principle of least privilege.
theory.explanation: A standard application deployment has separate directories for source code (`src`), configurations (`configs`), and backup logs (`backups`). Placing these inside a single parent structure makes path mappings clean. Assigning the whole tree to a restricted service user account prevents regular users from modifying configurations settings.
theory.why_exists: Minimizes blast radius; if the web server is compromised, the attacker cannot read files outside the designated folders.
theory.where_used: Cloud server deployments, container templates config, platform integrations.
theory.real_world_example: Setting application directories to `/var/www/my-app` owned by user `www-data`.
theory.best_practices: Ensure backup directories are read-only for standard application processes to prevent modifications of history log logs.
theory.common_mistakes: Running production deployment workflows directly using the root account, exposing the whole server to risks.

theory.title: Automation Log Rotators
theory.definition: A script or daemon utility that truncates or archives active log files to prevent storage depletion.
theory.explanation: If a server process writes logs indefinitely, it will eventually consume 100% of the disk, crash the database, and freeze the system. A log rotator script periodically backs up the active logs, clears the file (truncation), and stores history compressions. Truncating a file can be done by echoing an empty string: `echo '' > app.log`.
theory.why_exists: Guarantees continuous system uptime by keeping disk space consumption within bounded boundaries.
theory.where_used: Sysadmin cron automation schedules, container logs configurations.
theory.real_world_example: A logrotate cron job clearing `/var/log/nginx/access.log` daily.
theory.best_practices: Always archive active logs to a separate backup drive before truncating.
theory.common_mistakes: Deleting active log files (which breaks the process holding the descriptor) instead of truncating them.

interactive_examples.objective: Recursively assign directory ownership to a service user account.
interactive_examples.explanation: Sets devops_admin as the owner of capstone and all its child files recursively.
interactive_examples.tips: Always run ls -la to check if the owner column shows the new service user name.

interactive_examples.objective: Launch a process in the background and redirect output to a log.
interactive_examples.explanation: Spawns the app.sh script in the background, piping stdout to app.log and keeping terminal active.
interactive_examples.common_mistakes: Forgetting the ampersand, which locks your foreground session.
interactive_examples.tips: Ensure the target log file has write permission before starting the background redirection.

lessons.title: 1. Create Capstone Workspace
lessons.definition: The Capstone Project combines directories setup, variables configuration, and permissions.
lessons.explanation: Create nested subfolders inside capstone to structure source codes, variables configurations, and backup stores.
lessons.instruction: Create directories: capstone/src, capstone/configs, and capstone/backups using mkdir.

lessons.title: 2. Create Server Configuration
lessons.definition: Configuration files store system parameters, environment variables, and ports settings.
lessons.explanation: Use echo to populate a simple server.conf file mapping PORT=8080.

lessons.title: 3. Create Application Script
lessons.definition: Shell scripts automate system operations, status reporting, and background processes.
lessons.explanation: Create app.sh that prints status outputs, loop delays, and log paths.
lessons.instruction: Write shebang and a startup message into capstone/src/app.sh.
lessons.hint: Type: echo -e '#!/bin/bash\necho "App starting on port 8080..."' > capstone/src/app.sh

lessons.title: 4. Set Execution Permissions
lessons.definition: Execution flags allow scripts to run directly from shell pathways.
lessons.explanation: Add execute (+x) permission flag to the application script.

lessons.title: 5. Create Administration Account
lessons.definition: Administrative operations require dedicated restricted service accounts.
lessons.explanation: Add a new simulation system user account named devops_admin.
lessons.instruction: Add a new system user named devops_admin. (Use simulated useradd)

lessons.title: 6. Reassign Workspace Ownership
lessons.definition: Securing configurations requires assigning file ownership to the service user.
lessons.explanation: Use chown to reassign the capstone folder tree owner to devops_admin.
lessons.instruction: Recursively assign ownership of the capstone folder to devops_admin.

lessons.title: 7. Lock Configuration Secrets
lessons.definition: Configuration values containing passwords or ports should be owner-read only.
lessons.explanation: chmod 600 restricts access to only the file owner, hiding parameters from other system users.

lessons.title: 8. Write Archival Script
lessons.definition: Backup scripts copy source structures into compressed storage archives.
lessons.explanation: Create backup.sh containing simple files cloning steps.
lessons.instruction: Write shebang and copy instructions to capstone/src/backup.sh.

lessons.title: 9. Script Executable Flag
lessons.definition: Backup utilities must be executable to run inside automation cron loops.
lessons.explanation: Add execution permission flag to backup.sh.

lessons.title: 10. Test System Backup
lessons.definition: Validating backups before deployment checks copy scripts syntax.
lessons.explanation: Run backup.sh command to copy source directories into backup folders.
lessons.instruction: Run the backup script using the bash interpreter.

lessons.title: 11. Verify Network Ports Bindings
lessons.definition: Verify active port allocations using ss or netstat.
lessons.explanation: Confirm which socket ports are active and open.
lessons.instruction: Display active TCP listening socket routes.

lessons.title: 12. Create Log Rotator
lessons.definition: Log rotation clears old outputs preventing disk depletion.
lessons.explanation: Write rotation scripts to empty log files periodically.
lessons.instruction: Create capstone/src/rotate.sh containing echo command clearing app logs.

lessons.title: 13. Deploy Application Daemon
lessons.definition: Running scripts in background launches them as persistent services.
lessons.explanation: Launch app.sh redirecting outputs to capstone/app.log.
lessons.instruction: Launch capstone/src/app.sh in background, redirecting stdout to capstone/app.log.

lessons.title: 14. Inspect Active Log Output
lessons.definition: tail views trailing parts of active logs to diagnose startup issues.
lessons.explanation: Prints log tail configurations containing application output lines.
lessons.instruction: Inspect active log output of capstone/app.log.

lessons.title: 15. Complete Capstone Review
lessons.definition: Final validation of running background configurations and variables settings.
lessons.explanation: Run process audit to check background execution is active.
lessons.instruction: Expose background jobs list status to verify app completion.

exercises.title: Full system backup automation
exercises.problem: Create a scheduled backup task that copies your config files to the backup drive every hour.
exercises.hint: Use a cron expression or a while loop containing sleep.
exercises.expected_result: Autosave backup file instances visible in backups/.

exercises.title: Reassign and inspect permissions
exercises.problem: Check chown modifications results recursively to ensure all subfiles are restricted.
exercises.expected_result: Owner show as devops_admin for all directories.

exercises.title: Simulate log rotation failures
exercises.problem: Test log rotator output if target log path does not exist, and write error logs.
exercises.expected_result: Outputs error logs when missing directories are tested.
exercises.objective: Integrate error handlers inside automation utilities.

exercises.title: Audit active network ports
exercises.problem: Inspect listening sockets on the host before and after starting your app daemon.
exercises.expected_result: Port 8080 binds only when daemon script is running.

exercises.title: Background daemon validation
exercises.problem: Audit variables export parameters mapping within a running background job.
exercises.expected_result: Confirm PID matches active jobs list.

exercises.title: Secure workspace cleanup
exercises.problem: Safely archive all configuration files before truncating workspace blocks.
exercises.hint: Run cp backups before executing deletions.
exercises.expected_result: Files successfully backed up before cleanup.

quiz.question: What is the primary role of the Capstone project?
options: Integrate file operations, permissions, scripting, and networking into a deployment scenario
quiz.answer: Integrate file operations, permissions, scripting, and networking into a deployment scenario
quiz.explanation: The capstone aggregates all sysadmin skills to establish a production environment from scratch.

incorrect_explanations.Deploy and scale clusters: This is a Kubernetes task, not a basic Linux capstone.
incorrect_explanations.Configure AWS networks gateways: This is a cloud engineer path module.
incorrect_explanations.Write frontend frameworks pages: DevLab frontend relies on Next.js pages, not sysadmin commands.

quiz.question: What permission setting is set on config files to ensure they are owner-read only?
quiz.explanation: 600 (owner read/write) blocks all group and public reads, protecting access keys.

incorrect_explanations.777: Permits public writes and is highly insecure.
incorrect_explanations.755: Allows everyone to read and execute the file.
incorrect_explanations.444: Makes the file read-only for everyone, including the owner.

quiz.question: Which flag is appended to chown to recursively modify ownership inside folders?
quiz.explanation: -R is the standard recursive flag for ownership and permission changes.

quiz.question: How do you run a shell script in the background while redirecting stdout logs?
quiz.explanation: > output.log redirects stdout, and & tells the shell to run it in the background.

incorrect_explanations.bash script.sh >> output.log: Runs script in the foreground, locking the terminal.
incorrect_explanations.bash script.sh & > output.log: Syntax is invalid since the ampersand should be at the end.
incorrect_explanations.systemctl start script.sh: systemctl only runs configured system services scripts, not raw path files.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of log rotation?
options: Prevent disk space depletion by archiving/truncating log files
Transmit log outputs to database tables
quiz.answer: Prevent disk space depletion by archiving/truncating log files
quiz.explanation: Active logs are truncated or rotated to keep storage consumption within boundary configurations.

incorrect_explanations.Increase application execution speed: Has no effect on execution speed.
incorrect_explanations.Transmit log outputs to database tables: Rotators write to file storage, not DB.

quiz.question: Which command is used to inspect active TCP socket binds?
quiz.explanation: ss -lnt lists all active TCP listening sockets.

quiz.question: True or False: Deleting a log file is always identical to truncating it using `echo > file`.
quiz.explanation: Deleting standard logs breaks the process holding the file descriptor, whereas truncating clears contents safely.

incorrect_explanations.True: Deleting files causes log write failures until process restarts.

quiz.question: Which directory commonly stores deployment executable script source files?
quiz.explanation: src/ (source) is the standard folder for project binaries and script files.

incorrect_explanations.configs/: Used for configuration files, not script source files.

quiz.question: What is the function of the `jobs` command in process controls?
options: Lists background tasks spawned by the current shell
quiz.answer: Lists background tasks spawned by the current shell
quiz.explanation: jobs lists active shell-controlled processes like sleep or bg tasks.

incorrect_explanations.Displays CPU cycles: top or ps displays CPU cycles.

quiz.question: Scenario: You deployed an application daemon in background redirecting to `app.log`. What command lets you audit the real-time startup lines?
quiz.explanation: tail -f follows the file in real-time as lines are appended, displaying startup logs.

incorrect_explanations.cat app.log: Prints static contents once, does not trace active updates.
incorrect_explanations.ss -lntp: Audits active ports, does not output file log texts.
incorrect_explanations.ps aux: Audits active PIDs, does not display app console logs.

resources.summary: The Capstone project integrates nested directories creation, file permissions restriction chmod 600, owner delegation chown -R, background execution script redirects, port checks ss, and logs rotation script validations.
resources.cheat_sheet:
mkdir -p [dirs] - build paths
chmod 600 [file] - restrict permissions
chown -R [owner] [dir] - change owner recursively
bash [script].sh > [log] &
ss -lnt - check ports
jobs - check background jobs
revision_notes: Always secure secrets using chmod 600 before launching scripts.
Redirect stdout using > filename, and stderr using 2> filename.
Verify jobs status inside terminal to confirm daemon loops remain active.
beginner_mistakes: Forgetting to allocate execution permissions on backup.sh.
Deleting files instead of truncating them when working with active process descriptors.
best_practices: Create modular directories architectures for clean microservice separation.
Deploy background processes using restricted user accounts instead of root.

interview_questions.question: How do you verify if a process is running in the background?
interview_questions.answer: Run jobs command or ps aux grep searches.

interview_questions.question: How do you append both stdout and stderr to a log file?

additional_practice: Build a complete multi-user workspace secure configuration directory tree.
Write a backup cron job utility replicating deployment setups.

books.title: DevOps Handbook by Gene Kim
books.description: Core textbook for automation and system architectures.
